notes: 8-pair Games of the Week rotation. Pick: weekOfYear (ET) mod 8. Each pair = one game from Mind's Gambit Featured Games archive (men) + one women's chess game (PGNs supplied by Mourad from chessgames.com). All games verified, no fabrication.

men.players: Paul Morphy – Duke of Brunswick & Count Isouard
men.body: The most famous teaching game in chess history. Morphy, age twenty-one, plays the Duke and Count while watching a performance of Norma. In just seventeen moves he demonstrates the eternal principles of opening play: rapid development, central control, decisive use of the king's exposed position. Every chess teacher in the world has shown this game.

women.meta: European Women's Championship, Saint Petersburg 2009 · Nimzo-Indian Defence · 0–1
women.body: Round 10 of a 13-round event. Twenty-one-year-old Maryana Huda (rated 2240) plays a long fighting battle against Georgian master Sopio Gvetadze (2377). 137 points of rating separate them. After sixty-eight moves of pure endgame technique, Maryana loses — but the record is what matters: she was there, at the top of European women's chess, in a thirteen-player Round 10 grind. This game now sits inside The Daily Gambit because her chess is part of ours.

men.kicker: Men · The Game of the Century
men.meta: Rosenwald Trophy, New York 1956 · Grünfeld Defence · 0–1
men.body: Bobby Fischer was thirteen years old. Donald Byrne was a U.S. champion. On move 17, Fischer played 17…Be6!! — offering his queen for a position of such positional and tactical power that Hans Kmoch christened it the Game of the Century. The Marshall Chess Club, New York, October 17, 1956.

women.meta: US Women's Championship, Chandler 1997 · Modern Benoni · ½–½
women.body: August 1997. Two future US Women's Champions meet in the Modern Benoni: thirteen-year-old Irina Krush (eventual eight-time US Women's Champion) and Jennifer Shahade at seventeen (eventual two-time US Women's Champion, later author of Chess Bitch). Forty-eight moves, fighting draw — and the early shape of two careers that would define American women's chess for the next thirty years.

men.meta: Hoogovens, Wijk aan Zee 1999 · Pirc Defence · 1–0
men.body: Kasparov hunts Topalov's king from b8 all the way to a3 in a 44-move attacking masterpiece. The 24.Rxd4!! rook sacrifice and the king-march that follows are widely considered one of the greatest combinations ever played in tournament chess. Round 4, January 20, 1999. Kasparov later called it one of the finest games of his career.

women.meta: Hoogovens, Wijk aan Zee 1998 · Sicilian Najdorf · 1–0
women.body: January 22, 1998 — one year before Kasparov's Pearl of Wijk, in the same tournament hall. Judit Polgar grinds Viswanathan Anand, then a world top-five player en route to his first FIDE title in 2000. A long, technical Najdorf — the kind of win that proved Polgar wasn't a one-game phenomenon but a permanent presence in the world elite. (PGN includes Stockfish 9 in-line analysis.)

men.meta: World Chess Championship, Singapore 2024 · Zukertort Opening · 0–1
men.body: December 12, 2024, Singapore. Game 14, match tied 6½–6½. A rook-and-bishop endgame that looked drawn for the defending champion. In time pressure on move 55, Ding plays 55.Rf2?? — a tragic miscalculation that allows Gukesh to trade pieces into a winning king-and-pawn endgame. Three moves later Ding resigned. Gukesh became the youngest undisputed World Chess Champion in history at eighteen years old.

women.meta: Polgar–Spassky match, Budapest 1993 · Ruy Lopez Closed · 1–0
women.body: February 1, 1993, Budapest. Sixteen-year-old Judit Polgar plays a ten-game match against Boris Spassky, the tenth World Champion (1969–72), then fifty-six. She wins 5½–4½. This is Round 2 — the first decisive game of the match. The youngest of the Polgar sisters announces herself to the world by beating a former World Champion in his own city. (PGN includes Stockfish 9 in-line analysis.)

men.body: A casual game played during the London tournament of 1851 that became the symbol of Romantic chess. Anderssen sacrifices both rooks, a bishop, and his queen, then delivers mate with the three minor pieces he has left. Played without clocks, for honor, against the era's tactical master.

women.meta: 5th Soviet Team Championship Final, Vilnius 1958 · Ruy Lopez Closed · 1–0
women.body: July 9, 1958, Vilnius. Seventeen-year-old Nona Gaprindashvili plays in the Soviet Team Championship Final — the highest level of team chess in the world at the time. Four years later she would dethrone Elisaveta Bykova to become the fifth Women's World Champion, beginning a sixteen-year reign that ended only when Maia Chiburdanidze took the title in 1978.

men.kicker: Men · The Machine That Taught Itself
men.meta: DeepMind paper, London 2017 · Queen's Indian Defence · 1–0
men.body: December 2017. DeepMind's AlphaZero — four hours of self-play, no human chess knowledge — crushes Stockfish 8 (the strongest classical engine of its day) by 28 wins to 0 with 72 draws. This is Game 10 of the published sample: a knight sacrifice on h6 to expose the king, then patient piece restriction rather than immediate material recovery. The game that pointed chess forward into the neural-network era.

women.kicker: Women · India's First Female GM Beats a Male GM
women.meta: Abu Dhabi Open 2007 · King's Indian Fianchetto · 1–0
women.body: Abu Dhabi, August 20, 2007. Humpy Koneru (2572) — India's first female grandmaster, earned in 2002 at fifteen years and one month — defeats Ukrainian GM Evgenij Miroshnichenko, rated 99 points higher at 2671. Koneru went on to be the second-highest-rated woman in history (after Polgar) and a two-time World Rapid Champion (2019, 2024). A working-class champion who has stayed in the women's top five for two decades while raising a family.

men.body: Named 'Evergreen' by Wilhelm Steinitz — a game whose beauty would never fade. Anderssen sacrifices his queen with 19.Rad1!! to set up an irresistible mating net. The companion piece to the Immortal Game, and proof that the same player could conjure brilliance twice.

women.meta: 1st Hangzhou Women's GM Tournament 2011 · Sicilian Canal-Sokolsky · 1–0
women.body: July 15, 2011, Hangzhou. Reigning Women's World Champion Hou Yifan (2575) plays a textbook attacking masterpiece against French No. 1 Marie Sebag (2510). Open lines on the kingside, sustained pressure, and a clean conversion ending in 32.f8=Q+ for mate next move. Hou had been WWC for just one year — she would hold the title four times in seven years. (PGN includes Stockfish 9 in-line analysis.)

men.kicker: Men · Polgar Breaks the Glass Ceiling
men.meta: Russia vs The Rest of the World, Moscow 2002 · Ruy Lopez Berlin · 1–0
men.body: September 9, 2002. Garry Kasparov is the world's #1 rated player at 2838. Judit Polgar defeats him with the white pieces in the Berlin Defence — cited in every chess history as the moment the highest barrier in the men's game was broken. Round 5 of the Russia vs The Rest of the World match in Moscow. We include this game in the men's column because of who Kasparov was, and what his loss meant.

women.meta: Norway Chess Armageddon (Women), Oslo · May 25, 2026 · Réti · 0–1
women.body: Eight days ago. The classical game ended drawn; in the Armageddon tiebreak, seventeen-year-old Indian phenomenon Divya Deshmukh (with draw odds as Black) outlasts reigning Women's World Champion Ju Wenjun in a 51-move endgame battle. The same tournament currently dominating The Daily Gambit's lead column. Heart rates above 160 beats per minute — exactly the kind of contest WGM Maryana Huda's 2025 research paper analyzed.
